story:
On the hottest day of the year, players huddled under the lone shade tree until the final burst of cheers sent everyone racing from the shade. The winning run slid across home plate, and the team leapt to its feet, crowned Champs. All but one. He hung back, quietly packing his bat and glove, tugging his cap low to hide tears he couldn’t stop. He wiped his dust-streaked face, walked out to high five his teammates, accepted his medal with a nod, and stood tall for the photo—while knowing, deep down, he never wanted to play baseball again.

It hadn’t started that way. When summer rolled in, he had been giddy. He laid out his uniform the night before the first game—white with blue trim, his town on the front, his name stitched proudly across the back, favorite number below. He decided on pants just below the knees and bright blue socks pulled high. He could see it all: sun on his shoulders, the crack of a bat, ice cream after the win. After weeks of practice, he ran onto the field ready. The first games were pure joy. He swung hard, threw with abandon, laughed with his friends. No home runs, a few strikeouts, but it was the best summer ever. Until it wasn’t.

As the team kept winning big, everything shifted. The batting order stopped changing, and he was almost always last. He begged silently to try every position, especially the infield, but rarely left the outfield grass. Then came the worst blow: he sat more innings than he played. No explanation. He was afraid to ask. He decided he must have done something terribly wrong, though he didn’t know what. Panic rose each time he stepped to the plate. The fear of striking out made striking out more likely. When he finally took the field, fear made his hands clumsy. He prayed the season would end. As he sat down again, his coach said, “I know you pray for the season to end because you think you’ve done something terribly wrong. Stay ready.”

You see, he wasn’t some teenager fighting for a spot on a high school roster. He was a sweet, spunky nine-year-old who had just figured out how to tie his shoes. When he wasn’t dreaming of home runs, he was riding his bike in the woods, playing backyard football with his brothers, and sprinting through sprinklers. The giddy feeling of summer baseball—friends, sun, and ice cream win or lose—faded. A little piece of his childhood quietly slipped away.

Years passed. The boy became a man who still loved the Great American Pastime. He carried no bitterness, only a small, lasting sting for the nine-year-old who had lost his joy. He loved the game, just not playing it.

In time, he became a dad. His little boy idolized him, wanted to be just like him, and asked every evening to play catch until the ball disappeared into dusk. The dad taught him every position, from catcher to center field. When his son asked to join a real team—with a uniform and his name on the back—the dad hesitated, fearing the old wound would reopen in a new heart. But he recognized that giddy light in his son’s eyes. He couldn’t deny him. He said yes—with one condition: he would be the coach.

The sadness of that long-ago season faded under his son’s joy. Now, as a man, he understood the lesson. He would coach every player with kindness and patience, even if it meant losing a few games. He couldn’t shield his son from every disappointment, but for this one short summer, he would try to give each child the best season ever.
